title: 第 18 節
en: It was that vast domain of surprise, of terror, of marvel, and miracle, the unknown, as distinguished 120 LECTURE VI.

from the known, or, as I like to express it, the infinite, as distinct from the finite, which supplied from the earliest times the impulse to religious thought and language, though in the beginning these thoughts and names had little of what we now call religions about them. You remember that the very name of deva in Sanskrit, of dens in Latin, which afterwards became the name of God, meant originally bright, and no more. It came to mean God after a long process of evolution, which took place even before the Aryan separation, and of which we can only just catch the last glimpses in the phraseology of the Vedic poets.
Ag-ni, rire, as one of the Devas.

How this came about we shall, I think, best learn to understand if we anal^^se the growth of one of the many Devas or gods who form the Pantheon of the Veda. Many of these Vedic Devas appear likewise under more or less puzzling disguises in the mythology and religion of the other Aryan nations. Some, however, exist in the Veda only as real Devas, while we find no trace of them, as mythological or divine beings, in other countries of the Aryan world. I shall begin my analysis of Physical Religion with a Deva, belonging to this latter class, with the god of fire, called Agni in the Veda, but unknown under that name in any of the other Aryan mythologies, though the word agni, in the sense of fire, occurs in Latin as ignis, in Lituanian as ugnl, in old Slavonic as ogni.

When I say the god of fire, I use an expression which has become familiar to us from classical mythology. We speak of a god of the sky, or of the wind, or of the rain. But you will see that in the PHYSICAL RELIGION. 121 Veda we can watch this god of fire long before he is a god at all ; and, on the other hand, we shall be able to trace his further growth till he is no longer a god of fire merely, but a supreme god, a god above all other gods, a creator and ruler of the world.

In fact we shall learn to understand by this one instance the authentic history of that long psychological process which, beginning with the simplest and purely material perceptions, has led the human mind to that highest concept of deity which we have inherited together with our language, as members of the great Aryan, and not of the Semitic family.
Early conceptions of Fire.

If you can for a moment transfer yourselves to that early stage of life to which we must refer not only the origin, but likewise the early phases of Physical Religion, you can easily understand what an impression the first appearance of Fire must have made on the human mind. Fire was not given as something permanent or eternal, like the sky, or the earth, or the water. In whatever way it first appeared, whether through lightning or through the friction of the branches of trees, or through the sparks of flints, it came and went, it had to be guarded, it brought destruction, but at the same time it made life possible in winter, it served as a protection during the night, it became a weapon of defence and offence, and last, not least, it changed man from a devourer of raw flesh into an eater of cooked meat. At a later time it became the means of working metal, of making tools and weapons, it became an indispensable factor in all mechanical and artistic progress, 122 LECTUEE VI.
and has remained so ever since. What should we be without lire even now"?
The etymological meauingf of Agni.

What then did the early Aryas think of it, or, what is the same, how did they name it? Its oldest name in Sanskrit is Agni, and this has been preserved in Latin as ignis, in Lituanian as ngin, in old Slavonic as ogni. It was therefore a very old name. So far as we can venture to interpret such ancient names, Agni seems to have expressed the idea of quickly moving, from a root AG or AG, to drive. The nearest approach would be the Latin ag-ilis. Another Sanskrit name for fire is vah-ni, and this, too, coming from the same root which we have in veho and vehemens, would have meant originally what moves about quickly. In the Veda Agni is called raghupatvan, quickly flying (X. 6, 4).
Names of Fire.

It will be useful to examine some more of the old names of fire, because every one of them, if we can still interpret it etymologically, wiU enable us to see in how many different ways fire was conceived by the Aryas, how it struck them, what they thought of it, Dahana means simply the burner.

r"^ Anal a, from an, to breathe, would seem to mean \ the breathing, or blowing fire, just as anila is a \ name for wind. The root AN, to breathe, is the V \ same which we have in animus, anima, and in Greek av\ios. In the Veda the fire is often said to I be breathing (abhi-svasan, I. 140^, 5).
PHYSICAL RELIGION. 123 Pavaka, a frequent name of Agni, conveys the meaning of cleaning, clearing, illuminating. Some scholars have derived irvp and fire from the same root.

Tanunapat is a Vedic name of Agni. It is explained as meaning ' offspring of himself.' It is possible, no doubt, to conceive Agni as self-born. He is called sva-yoni in the Mahabharata (19, 13931). But the usual idea in the Veda is that he has a father and mother, namely, the two fire-sticks.
(ratavedas, another name for Agni, means allseeing, all-knowing, like visvavedas.
Vaisvanara seems to convey the meaning of kept by all men, or useful and kind to a]l, universal.

Another epithet applied to Agni is Bhura^iyu. Bhurar/yu means quick, and is. formed on the same lines as Agni and Vahni. Derived as it is from a root BHAR, to bear, to carry, it seems to have meant originally, carried along headlong, borne away, or possibly, bearing away, like the Greek </)epo/x62^oj. This Sanskrit word bhura^iyu is almost the same word as the Greek 't>opcoi'vs, who is supposed to have brought to men the gift of fire, and to have become the founder of cities (Paus. ii. 15, 5)^.
Pire, named as active.

We ourselves occupy, of course, a totally different position from those who had first to conceive and to name fire. We learn the name mechanically from our parents, and the sound fire is a mere outward sign for what burns and hurts, or warms and cheers us. In after life we may learn to call fire with the ' Kuhn, Mythologische Studi^n, i. p. 211.

ancient Greek philosophers one of the four elements ; and, later on, a study of natural philosophy may teach us that fu*e consists of luminous and calorific rays, that it is a natural force, or, it may be, a motion of something unknown which ^\G call ether. But in all this we deal with predicates only, and the underlying substance remains as unknown as the underlying agent whom the, as yet, undivided Aryas called simply Agni, the mover.

At all events w^e may well understand that the early inhabitants of the earth were puzzled by the fire. There was nothing like it in the whole world — now visible, now invisible, tangible, yet dangerous to touch, destroying whole forests and the habitations of men, and yet most welcome on the hearth, most cheerful in winter.

We can well understand how, after the senses had once taken note of this luminous apparition in its ever-varying aspects, a desire arose in the human mind, and in the human mind only, to know it ; to know it, not simply in the sense of seeing or feeling it, but to know it in the sense of conceiving and naming it, which is a very different thing.

How could that be done ? I cannot explain here once more the whole of the process of conceiving and naming, or naming and conceiving. You will find that subject treated in my first course of Gifford Lectures, and more fully in my work On the Science of Thought, published in 1887.

I can here only state it as a fact that the only instruments by which man could achieve this process of naming were what we call roots, and that all these roots, owing to the manner in which they first came into PHYSICAL RELIGION. 125 existence, expressed actions, the ordinary actions performed by men in an early state of society. There were roots expressive of striking, pushing, carrying, binding, lifting, squeezing, rubbing, and all the rest, and with these roots all that we now call namino* and conceivino-, the whole of our language, the whole of our thought, has been elaborated.

This is a fact, simply a fact, and not a mere theory. To doubt it, as has been done of late again, is to doubt the laws of thought. We may differ as to the exact form in which those roots existed from the first. Such doubts are allowable with regard to roots, as elements of speech, they are allow^able w^ith regard to letters, as the elements of sound, nay even with regard to the chemical elements, as constituting the whole material world. But to doubt the existence of any of these three classes of elements is either ignorance or unreason.

No one denies that we name and conceive by means of signs. These signs might have been anything, but, as a matter of fact, they were sounds ; and again, as a matter of fact, these sounds were what in the Science of Lanoruasre we call roots. When we examine these roots, as the actual elements of speech, we find that they signify acts, and we conclude that their sound was originally the involuntary clamor concomitans of the simplest acts of man. This last conclusion may no doubt be called an hypothesis only, and I have never represented it as anything else ; but, till a better hypothesis has been suggested, I retain it as the bcvSt working hypothesis.
If then the Aryas possessed a root, such as AG, by wdiich they expressed their own acts of marching, J 126 LECTURE VI.

running, jumping, and,, at last, moving in general, all they did in naming and conceiving the marching, running, jumping, or quickly moving luminous appearances of fire, was to say to each of them : ' Moving here,' 'Moving there,' or in Sanskrit Ag-ni-s^.
zh: 那是一個廣袤的領域，充滿驚奇、恐怖、奇觀與奇蹟——即未知者，與已知者相區別。

自已知之物而來，或者，如我樂於表達的方式，自無限者而來——無限者有別於有限者，它自上古以來即為宗教思想與語言提供了推動力，雖然最初這些思想與名稱在我們現今所謂的宗教中所佔的份量甚少。你還記得梵文中 deva 與拉丁文中 dens 這個名字本身吧，後來它成為了神（God）的名稱，但原意不過是「光明」而已。經過漫長的演化過程，它才逐漸有了神的含義——這個過程甚至早於雅利安人的分化而發生，我們只能在吠陀詩人的措辭中勉強窺見其最後的蹤跡。
阿格尼（Ag-ni）、里雷（rire）作為提婆之一。

若欲深入瞭解此事之經過，我認為最良好之方法，乃是剖析吠陀萬神殿中眾多提婆或諸神之一的演化過程。許多吠陀提婆在其他雅利安民族的神話及宗教中，同樣以或多或少令人費解的面貌出現。然而，有些提婆僅在吠陀中作為真實的提婆而存在，而在雅利安世界的其他地區，我們則尋不著其作為神話或神聖存在物之痕跡。我將以屬於此後一類別的提婆開始我對物質宗教的剖析，即火神，在吠陀中稱為阿耆尼（Agni），然而在任何其他雅利安神話中皆不以此名稱而聞；雖然 agni 一詞在火之意義上，在拉丁文中作 ignis 出現，在立陶宛文中作 ugnl 出現，在古斯拉夫文中作 ogni 出現。
當我說火神時，我使用了一個從古典神話對我們而言已變得熟悉的表達方式。我們談論天空之神、風神或雨神。但您將看到，在吠陀中，我們可以在這位火神尚未成為神祇之前的很早階段就觀察到他；另一方面，我們將能夠追蹤他的進一步發展，直到他不再僅僅是火神，而是至高無上的神祇——一位凌駕於所有其他神祇之上的神、世界的創造者和統治者。
事實上，我們將通過這一例證來了解那漫長心理過程的真實歷史，該過程始於最簡單、純粹物質性的知覺，引領人類心靈達至我們作為偉大雅利安家族（而非閃語族）成員，連同我們的語言一起所繼承的最高神聖概念。
火的早期觀念

如果你能暫時將自己置身於人類生命的早期階段——我們不僅必須參照此階段以尋求物質宗教（Physical Religion）的起源，而且要理解其早期的各個階段——那麼你便能輕易領會火焰首次出現對人類心靈所產生的深刻印象。火焰並非像天空、大地或水那樣被給予為某種永恆或持久的東西。無論它最初如何出現——無論是通過閃電、樹枝的摩擦，抑或燧石的火花——它來了又去了，它必須被守護，它帶來破壞，但同時它使人類在冬季得以生存，它在夜間提供保護，它成為防禦與進攻的武器，最後也是最重要的，它將人類從生肉的吞食者轉化為熟肉的食用者。在後來的時代，它成為冶金的手段，製造工具和武器，它成為一切機械和藝術進步中不可或缺的因素。
並且自此以後便一直如此。如果現在沒有火，我們會是什麼呢？
（備註：此標題下無正文內容提供翻譯。若有後續段落，請提供完整的英文原文以進行翻譯。）

那麼早期雅利安人對它有什麼看法，換句話說，他們如何稱呼它？它在梵文中最古老的名稱是阿格尼（Agni），這個詞被保存在拉丁文中為 ignis，在立陶宛文中為 ngin，在古斯拉夫文中為 ogni。因此它是一個非常古老的名稱。就我們對這類古代名稱的詮釋而言，阿格尼似乎表達了快速運動的概念，源自於語根 AG 或 AJ，意為「驅動」。最接近的對應詞是拉丁文的 ag-ilis。梵文中火的另一個名稱是瓦赫尼（vah-ni），這個詞也來自於同一個語根，我們在 veho 和 vehemens 中可以看到，其本義應為「快速移動的東西」。在《吠陀》中，阿格尼被稱為 raghupatvan（快速飛行）（X.6,4）。

Among the Aryans, one of the most sacred and most frequently invoked deities was Fire. Fire, in its various manifestations, appeared under different names; and it is most instructive to trace the history of these names, and to observe how the human mind, when brought into contact with the same natural phenomenon, creates different expressions for it, and how these expressions, after being used for ages, become mythological characters, with their own legends and genealogies. 在雅利安人當中，火是最神聖且最經常被祈禱的提婆之一。火在其種種顯現中，以不同的名稱出現；追溯這些名稱的歷史是極具啟發性的，我們可以觀察到人類的心智在接觸同一自然現象時，如何創造了不同的表達方式，以及這些表達方式在經歷了數個時代的使用後，如何演變為具有各自傳說與譜系的神話人物。 The Fire-god, among the Hindoos, is called **Agni**. This name is supposed to be derived from a root, signifying *to burn* or *to move*. In the Rig-Veda, Agni is invoked in more than 200 hymns; and even now, in India, fire is still religiously worshipped by the Hindoos in their daily rites and ceremonies. 在印度人當中，火神被稱為**阿格尼** (Agni)。這個名稱據信源自一個表示「燃燒」或「運動」的字根。在《梨俱吠陀》中，阿格尼在超過兩百首讚歌中被祈禱；甚至到了今日，在印度，火仍然由印度人在其日常儀式與典禮中被虔敬地崇拜。 The same deity, in the Zend-Avesta, the sacred books of the ancient Persians, is called **Atar**. This name also signifies *fire*. The Persian Fire-god, like the Indian Agni, was surrounded with all the poetical imagery which the human imagination could devise. The sacred fire was carried in procession; temples were erected for its worship; and even now, in the mountains of Persia, the eternal fire is said to burn, as a monument to that ancient faith. 同一位提婆在《阿維斯陀》（古代波斯人的聖典）中被稱為**阿塔爾** (Atar)。這個名稱也表示「火」。波斯火神阿塔爾如同印度的阿格尼一樣，被各種人類想像力所能構思的詩意意象所環繞。聖火在遊行中被運載；廟宇被建立用於其崇拜；甚至到了今日，據說在波斯的山區，永恆之火仍在燃燒，作為那遠古信仰的紀念碑。 The root of the word **Atar** is supposed to be **ata**, which occurs in the Greek *athenai*, and in the Latin *ardere*. Thus we discover in three different languages—Sanskrit, Zend, and Greek—different names for the same object, all derived from one common root. **阿塔爾**這個詞的字根據信是 **ata**，它出現在希臘文的 *athenai* 以及拉丁文的 *ardere* 中。因此我們在三種不同的語言中──梵文、中古波斯文與希臘文──發現了同一對象的不同名稱，全部源自一個共同的字根。
檢視古代火的更多名稱將會有所幫助，因為其中每一個名稱，如果我們仍能從詞源學上詮釋它，都將使我們看到雅利安人以多少種不同的方式構想火、火如何觸動他們、他們如何思考它。Dahana 僅僅意味著「燃燒者」。
Anal a 源自 an（呼吸）一詞，似乎意為「呼吸」或「吹火」，正如 anila 是風的一個名稱。根 AN（呼吸）與我們在 animus、anima 以及希臘文 ἄνεμος 中所見者相同。在吠陀中，火經常被說成是呼吸的（abhi-svasan, I. 140^, 5）。
物質宗教。123 帕瓦卡（Pavaka）是阿格尼（Agni）的常見名稱，傳達著清潔、澄清、照亮的含義。有些學者認為 irvp 與火（fire）源自同一語根。
Tanunapat 是梵語中阿耆尼（Agni）的一個吠陀名稱。它被解釋為意謂「他自己的後裔」。誠然，可以想像阿耆尼是自生的。在《摩訶婆羅多》（19, 13931）中，他被稱為自我之源（sva-yoni）。但吠陀中通常的觀念是，他有父親和母親，即兩根鑽火木。
（梨陀吠陀、阿格尼的另一個名稱，意為「遍見者」、「遍知者」，如毗濕婆吠陀所示。
Vaisvanara 一詞似乎傳達了「為眾人所守護、對眾人有益且友善、普遍適用」（bhagavata kept by all men, or useful and kind to all, universal）的含義。

應用於阿格尼（Agni）的另一個別名是跋羅逸烏（Bhura^iyu）。跋羅（Bhurar/yu）意為迅速，其構詞方式與阿格尼（Agni）和伐多尼（Vahni）相同。既然它源自根詞BHAR，意為承載、攜帶，它似乎原本的意思是被匆促地攜帶、被帶走，或者可能是攜帶而去，類似於希臘文φέροντος（phérontos）。這個梵文詞跋羅逸烏（bhura^iyu）幾乎就是希臘文 Φορωνεύς（Phoroneús）這個詞，此人據說曾將火的禮物帶給人類，並成為城市的建立者（Paus. ii. 15, 5）。
我需要更多的上下文來進行準確的翻譯。您提供的英文原文「Pire, named as active」相當簡短，而且： 1. "Pire" 這個詞在穆勒的著作中並不是常見的術語，可能是特定脈絡中的專有名詞或技術用語 2. 缺少前後句子，難以判斷其確切含義和語法功能 請提供： - 完整的段落或更多上下文 - 該句子周圍的德文原文（如果有的話） 這樣才能提供忠實於原意的專業翻譯。
我們自己當然佔據了一個截然不同的位置，不同於那些首次需要構想並命名火的人。我們從父母那裡機械地學習這個名字，而「fire」這個聲音對於那些燃燒、傷害或溫暖、安慰我們的東西，不過是一個純粹的外在標誌。在往後的人生中，我們可能會學到用……來稱呼火¹ ¹ Kuhn, *Mythologische Studien*, i. p. 211.
古代希臘哲學家將其列為四大元素之一；而稍後，對自然哲學的研究可以教導我們，火由發光和發熱的光線所組成，它是一種自然力，或者可能是我們所謂的以太（ether）的某種未知物質的運動。然而在所有這一切當中，我們只是在處理謂詞而已，而潛在的實體仍然如同潛在的施動者一樣保持未知——那位尚未分化的雅利安人簡稱之為阿格尼（Agni）的施動者，即運動者。
無論如何，我們很容易理解，地球上的早期居民對火感到困惑。世界上沒有任何東西像火一樣——時而可見，時而隱形，有形體卻危險至極，足以摧毀整片森林和人類的住所，然而它在爐火中又最受歡迎，在冬日裡最令人感到欣慰。
我們完全可以理解，在感官曾經以種種不同的面貌注意到這道發光的異象之後，人心——唯有人心——中如何油然而生出一種欲望：要認識它；不僅是在看到或感受到它的意義上認識它，而是在領悟它、命名它的意義上認識它，這乃是一種迥然不同的事情。
這如何可能呢？我無法在此再次詳細說明整個概念化與命名，或命名與概念化的過程。各位可在我的第一系列吉福德講座（Gifford Lectures）中找到這個主題的討論，更完整的論述則見於我於一八八七年出版的著作《論思想科學》（*On the Science of Thought*）。
我在此只能陳述一個事實：人類用以完成這種命名過程的唯一工具就是我們所謂的**語根**，而所有這些語根，由於它們最初產生的方式，都表達了動作——即早期社會中人類所進行的尋常動作。有表達敲擊、推動、運載、束縛、舉起、擠壓、摩擦等等的語根，正是用這些語根，我們現在所謂的命名*與概念化*——我們整個語言、我們整個思想——才得以發展而成。
這是一個事實，單純的事實，而不是一個理論而已。如同近來有人所為，質疑這一點，就是質疑思想的規律。我們或許在那些根源從一開始就以何種確切形式存在這一點上有所不同。這樣的質疑對於根源而言是可允許的，因為它們是言語的要素；對於字母而言也是可允許的，因為它們是聲音的要素；甚至對於化學元素而言也是可允許的，因為它們構成整個物質世界。但是質疑這三類元素中任何一類的存在，要麼是無知，要麼是悖理。
沒有人否認我們藉由符號來命名與思考。這些符號本可以是任何東西，但事實上它們是聲音；而且，事實上，這些聲音正是我們在語言科學中所謂的詞根。當我們檢視這些詞根作為言語的實際單位時，我們發現它們表達的是行為，並據此推斷，其聲音在原初時乃是人類最簡單的行為所伴隨的非自願的喧聲。這最後的結論無疑可被稱為假說而已，而我也從未將其表述為別的；然而，在更好的假說被提出之前，我將其保留為最佳的工作假說。
如果雅利安人擁有一個字根，例如 AG，藉以表達他們自身的行進動作，
奔跑、跳躍，最後泛指運動一般，這一切他們在命名和構想火焰那光耀的行動、奔跑、跳躍或迅速移動的顯現時所做的，無非是對每一個都說：「在這裡運動」、「在那裡運動」，或用梵文表示為 Ag-ni-s^。
